content.problemStatement:
Rohit dreams he is in a shop with an infinite amount of marbles. He is allowed to select nnn marbles. 

There are marbles of kkk different colors. From each color there are also infinitely many marbles. 

Rohit wants to have at least one marble of each color, but still there are a lot of possibilities for his selection. In his effort to make a decision he wakes up. 

Now he asks you how many possibilities for his selection he would have had. 

Assume that marbles of equal color can't be distinguished, and the order of the marbles is irrelevant.


Input Format
The first line of input contains a number T <= 100 that indicates the number of test cases to follow. 

Each test case consists of one line containing nnn and kkk, where nnn is the number of marbles Rohit selects and kkk is the number of different colors of the marbles.



Output Format
For each test case print the number of possibilities that Rohit would have had.
You can assume that this number fits into a signed 64 bit integer.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 100), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers n and k (1 ≤ k ≤ n ≤ 1,000,000).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing C(n‑1, k‑1), the number of possible selections. The result fits into a signed 64‑bit integer.

sampleTestCases.explanation: C(4,2)=6 ways to give at least one marble of each of the 3 colors when selecting 5 marbles.
When n = k, each color must appear exactly once, so only one possible selection.

solution.approach: The requirement of at least one marble of each colour transforms the problem into distributing the remaining (n‑k) indistinguishable marbles into k colour groups. By the stars‑and‑bars theorem the number of ways equals C((n‑k)+k‑1, k‑1) = C(n‑1, k‑1). Computing a single binomial coefficient can be done in O(min(r, n‑r)) time by iteratively multiplying and dividing, keeping the intermediate result integral. Since the answer is guaranteed to fit in 64‑bit, 64‑bit integer arithmetic is sufficient.
solution.timeComplexity: O(min(k‑1, n‑k)) per test case, O(T·min(k, n‑k)) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides input storage.
